expert.name: Eugene M. Schwartz
expert.core_thesis: Copy does not create desire. It channels pre-existing mass desire onto a specific product through strategically sequenced belief-building. The conversion power originates in the market, not in the writer.
key_questions: What is the dominant mass desire attachable to this product? (12 words or fewer)
Prospect's State of Awareness: Unaware / Problem Aware / Solution Aware / Product Aware / Most Aware?
Prospect's belief environment: what cannot be violated?
Product's mechanism and market familiarity with it?
Dominant objections: complexity, importance, price, trust?
operating_beliefs: Copy channels existing desire; it cannot create it.
Desire selection is the highest-leverage decision. Wrong desire = unrecoverable.
The headline does not sell. It stops discard and earns the next paragraph.
Each sentence sells the next. Persuasion is sequential.
The prospect's beliefs are immovable first-move constraints.
One fully believed promise beats many partially believed promises.
Write to the 'chimpanzee brain': vivid, concrete, gut-level. If praised AS writing, that's a warning signal.
Define product by what it DOES ('doeses'), not what it IS.
Powerful claims create disbelief unless the ad prepares and proves. Biography builds belief.
Ugly can outperform beautiful when sameness is the enemy.
biases: Favors long-form, copy-driven persuasion. May undervalue visual-first or design-driven conversion.
Treats copy as primary selling instrument. Underweights exogenous trust (brand, reviews, influencer).
Rewards mechanism-heavy copy. May overpenalize minimalist copy that converts via pre-built brand equity.
Assumes direct response context. Less reliable when copy is one touchpoint in multi-touch sequence.
limitations: Product UX / checkout friction. Strong copy does not guarantee conversion if interface fails.
Short-form social ads. Sentence-chain applies but platform dynamics (delivery, fatigue, scroll) are outside scope.
Micro-niche audiences. 'Mass desire' model may underrate viable niche plays with high LTV but tiny scope.
Multi-touch non-linear consumption. Single-piece Unaware-to-Most-Aware progression is less reliable when users bounce between assets.
Exogenous trust signals (reviews, influencer, platform trust) can dominate copy but are not modeled here.
Ultra-minimal pages for pre-aware audiences where belief sits outside the page.
Luxury/fashion copy that converts via vibe and brand equity without explicit mechanism or proof.

frameworks.name: Mass Desire Channeling
frameworks.purpose: What force already exists in the market that, if correctly aimed, will cause buying behavior?

mechanics.description: Mass desire = a private want spread publicly to enough people to form a market. Copy focuses it onto one product through one mechanism. Every product contains a physical product (the thing) and a functional product (what it DOES: its 'doeses'). Features are raw materials; performances are selling units.

components.name: Desire Identification
components.definition: The single dominant desire, statable in 12 words or fewer.
key_questions: Can you state the dominant desire in <=12 words? If not, copy fails focus.
Does every section tie back to it?
Does headline carry one desire or multiple?

components.name: Desire Power
components.definition: Three dimensions: urgency (demands satisfaction now), staying power (renews, resists satiation), scope (enough people at paying intensity).
key_questions: Rate urgency, staying power, scope. If weak on any, require compensating strength elsewhere.

components.name: Physical-to-Functional Translation
components.definition: Whether copy translates features into functional performances ('doeses') under the dominant desire.
key_questions: List all 'doeses.' Are they coherent under one desire or unrelated pile?
Is product defined by what it DOES, not IS?

decision_rules: IF copy attempts to 'create desire,' THEN penalize (channeling axiom violation).
IF multiple desires compete in headline/lead, THEN penalize scattered desire.
IF desire selection is wrong, THEN FATAL.
IF headline desire != body desire, THEN penalize 'desire breach.'
IF copy leads with physical attributes without desire linkage, THEN penalize.

frameworks.name: Five Stages of Awareness
frameworks.source: Breakthrough Advertising, 'Your Prospect's State of Awareness'
frameworks.purpose: What can you say first without being rejected, and what must the copy accomplish before asking for the sale?

mechanics.description: Copy must start where the prospect IS. Five stages define what the copy can safely say first and how direct it can be.

components.name: Unaware
components.definition: Prospect doesn't recognize the need. Headline must NOT sell. Enter via curiosity, story, acceptable imagery. Introduce problem indirectly.
components.red_flags: Opens with product/offer/selling to unaware audience.

components.name: Problem Aware
components.definition: Feels pain but doesn't know solution category. Headline names problem/consequences, promises relief (not product). Intensify problem before introducing solution.

components.name: Solution Aware
components.definition: Knows solutions exist, evaluating options. Headline positions better way via mechanism/unique angle. Differentiate via 'why this works when others fail.'

components.name: Product Aware
components.definition: Knows product, not convinced. Lead with advantage, offer, proof, risk reversal. Counter objections, make purchase safe.

components.name: Most Aware
components.definition: Knows product, wants it, needs trigger. Offer-forward: price, deadline, bonus, guarantee. Short and operational.

decision_rules: IF product name + price/offer opens but body must educate, THEN stage mismatch.
IF headline is 'deal oriented' but lead is 'educational,' THEN internal inconsistency.
IF copy introduces rejected-at-top claims without preparation, THEN penalize.
IF CTA before problem is established for early stages, THEN FATAL.
IF selling language hits Unaware/Problem Aware immediately, THEN FATAL.

frameworks.name: Five Stages of Market Sophistication
frameworks.purpose: Given competitive claim saturation, what type of claim will still feel fresh and believable?

mechanics.description: Sophistication = what the market has been promised, tried, and learned to distrust. Determines the primary selling lever.

components.name: Stage 1: First in Market
components.definition: Simple direct claim. Novelty carries. Penalize overcomplication.

components.name: Stage 2: Competition Appears
components.definition: Enlarge/dramatize the successful claim. Faster, easier, bigger. Specific amplification.

components.name: Stage 3: Claim Fatigue
components.definition: Need NEW MECHANISM to make familiar promise fresh. Must be new, believable, emotionally loaded.

components.name: Stage 4: Mechanism Saturation
components.definition: Elaborate/embellish mechanism, or explain why prior mechanisms fail. Deeper proof, more specificity.

components.name: Stage 5: Exhausted Market
components.definition: Shift from promise/mechanism to IDENTIFICATION. Identity-based entry. New problem framing or desire angle.

decision_rules: IF Stage 1 + complex mechanism, THEN penalize overcomplication.
IF Stage 3+ with old claim phrased plainly, THEN penalize stale.
IF Stage 5 with bigger promises instead of identification, THEN FATAL.
IF saturated market + pure generic benefit, THEN near-fatal.

frameworks.name: Verbalization: 38 Headline Strengthening Techniques
frameworks.purpose: Given the right claim, how to state it so it hits harder, feels fresher, earns the read.

mechanics.description: Verbalization strengthens a chosen claim by binding images, measurements, proof cues, or curiosity triggers. It is DOWNSTREAM of desire, awareness, and sophistication. Cannot fix strategic misalignment. 38 techniques grouped by function:

functional_groups.techniques: 1-3: Measure size, speed, comparison. Add specific numbers, quantified scale, relative measurement.
4-6: Metaphorize, sensitize (feel/see/hear), demonstrate with prime example.
7-9: Dramatize result, paradox, remove limitations ('without X, even if Z').
10-12: Associate with valued people, show breadth of work, state as question.
16-18: Stress newness, exclusivity, turn into challenge.
22-24: Connect mechanism to claim, startle by contradicting expected mechanism, connect need to claim.
functional_groups.example: 'Feeds waste gas fumes back into your engine'

functional_groups.techniques: 25-27: Offer information, case history, name the problem.
28-30: Warn pitfalls, emphasize by phraseology, show ease via overcome limitation.
31-34: State difference, show limitations overcome, address non-buyers, address directly.
functional_groups.example: 'To the man who will settle for nothing less...'

functional_groups.techniques: 35-38: Dramatize production difficulty, accuse of too good, challenge beliefs, Q&A headline.

decision_rules: IF low sophistication, THEN fewer layers needed. Penalize over-verbalization that obscures.
IF high sophistication, THEN verbalization required to freshen. Penalize plain claims.
IF headline introduces mechanism, THEN body must mechanize and prove it. Penalize name-drops without support.

frameworks.name: Seven Body Copy Techniques
frameworks.purpose: What body copy method transforms desire into belief and action?

mechanics.description: Seven techniques, each with a distinct job. Mix must match Awareness and Sophistication.

components.name: Intensification (13 Parts)
components.definition: Fresh fulfillments of dominant desire. NOT repeating same benefit in new adjectives.
components.thirteen_parts: 1) Direct claim presentation, 2) Claims in action, 3) Bring in reader/first-day demo, 4) Show how to test, 5) Stretch in time, 6) Bring in audience, 7) Expert approval, 8) Compare/contrast/superiority, 9) Picture the black side, 10) Show ease, 11) Metaphor/analogy, 12) Summarize (no repetition), 13) Guarantee as climax.
components.key_questions: Count parts present. Is each a genuinely new angle? Is there product-in-action, reader-as-actor, and testable demonstration?
components.red_flags: Same phrasing repeated, flat benefit list, 'fake variation' via adjective swaps.

components.name: Identification
components.definition: Build saleable personality/role into product. Product as instrument or acknowledgment of desired role (character roles or achievement roles).
components.key_questions: What role is offered? Character or achievement? Tied to product features or pasted on? Primary image respected or contradicted?

components.name: Gradualization
components.definition: Build belief BEFORE stating the claim. Chain of acceptances: start with what they accept, link each new claim inside accepted claim.
components.devices: Inclusion questions (force 'yes'), detailed identification qualifiers, contradiction of false beliefs (with authority), logic connectors ('because,' 'therefore'), syllogistic chains (A->B->C), if-then structures, paragraph parallelism.
components.key_questions: Map the 'belief ladder.' Is first acceptance a nod? Are bridge steps small? Where does proof appear relative to doubt? Are big claims embedded in accepted frames?

components.name: Redefinition
components.definition: Remove objections before prospect forms them. Three modes: Simplification (too complicated), Escalation (not important enough), Price Reduction (too expensive, shift comparison frame).
components.key_questions: What's the biggest objection? Pre-handled or ignored? Which mode? Backed by proof or mere wordplay?

components.name: Mechanization
components.definition: Verbal proof product does what it claims. Three stages: 1) Name mechanism (when known), 2) Describe mechanism (when unknown/tired, keep emotionally loaded), 3) Feature mechanism (when sophisticated or dramatic, elevate to headline).
components.key_questions: Can you isolate the mechanism phrase? Used as reason-why proof or jargon? Demonstrated in action? Correct stage for market? Emotionally loaded or sterile?
components.red_flags: Big claims + no mechanism/proof in proof-demanding context.

components.name: Concentration
components.definition: Destroy alternate paths to desire satisfaction. Expose competitor weakness while proving your solution avoids it. Not mere attack.
components.key_questions: Does copy neutralize alternatives (DIY, competitor, procrastination)? With evidence, not insults? Only attacks weaknesses it can solve?

components.name: Camouflage
components.definition: Borrow conviction via non-ad format. Three elements: Format (looks like editorial), Phraseology (sounds like the medium), Mood (understatement, sincerity, reduced 'ad language').
components.key_questions: Does piece match expected channel voice? Believability borrowed from credible structures or merely asserted? Format minimizes editorial-to-ad shift?

mechanics.technique_selection: Low awareness: heavier Intensification + Gradualization. High sophistication: heavier Mechanization + Concentration. High skepticism: Camouflage. Logical long-form sequence: Intensify -> Gradualize -> Mechanize -> Redefine -> Concentrate -> Close.

decision_rules: IF Most Aware + over-intensification, THEN penalize hype.
IF weak desire + abstract intensification, THEN penalize 'benefit list without vivid action.'
IF Stage 5 sophistication, THEN identification must lead. Penalize promise-only.
IF high-skepticism claim without Gradualization preparation or authority proof, THEN penalize.
IF mechanism becomes dry/technical/emotionless, THEN penalize.
IF Stage 3+ + same mechanism as competitors, THEN penalize.
IF copy doesn't answer 'why this way,' THEN prospect satisfies desire elsewhere.

frameworks.name: Final Touches and Execution Quality
frameworks.source: Breakthrough Advertising; Phillips Publishing lecture; The Brilliance Breakthrough (partial)
frameworks.purpose: Is the copy believable, absorbing, emotionally charged, vivid, and continuously pulling forward?

components.name: Verification (Proof Placement)
components.definition: Proof where the reader demands it. Short, dramatic, specific. 'Biography builds belief' for big claims.
components.key_questions: Identify each doubt point. Is proof right there? Specific and credible, or vague and dull?
components.red_flags: Proof contradicts itself or uses fake specificity.

components.name: Interweaving
components.definition: Blend emotion + image + logic in the same sentence. Every word carries image/feeling.
components.key_questions: Sample 5 random sentences. Each include at least one of: image, emotion, logic?

components.name: Momentum
components.definition: Forward-pulling transitions and teasers. Two types: momentum phrases (transitions) and incomplete statements (raise question, imply answer).
components.key_questions: Count transitions. Are open loops created and paid off? Does each section transition promise info, raise question, or imply payoff?

components.name: Mood and Simplicity
components.definition: Tone matches channel/audience. Write to 'chimpanzee brain': vivid, concrete, low-friction. Connectors ('because,' 'so,' 'which means') make logic obvious. Word pictures cause reader to generate mental images.
components.key_questions: Abstraction density: how many sentences contain concrete image vs abstract nouns? Can reader visualize promise in 3 seconds? Do connectors make logic effortless? Does reader ever have to reread?

decision_rules: IF big claims + proof absent/misplaced, THEN penalize heavily.
IF logic without imagery/emotion, THEN penalize interweaving failure.
IF no forward pull (no teasers, no questions), THEN penalize momentum failure.
IF 'salesy' tone in skeptical channel, THEN penalize mood mismatch.
IF 'smart sounding' jargon adding no persuasion function, THEN penalize.

vocabulary.mass_desire: Not 'interest.' Pre-existing force in the market. Copy channels it, cannot create it.
vocabulary.doeses: Schwartz's term for the multiple functional performances one product delivers. Each is a potential selling angle.
vocabulary.sophistication: NOT intelligence. Market's exposure to and fatigue from similar promises/mechanisms.
vocabulary.mechanism: The 'why it works' that makes promise believable. Not trivia. Must be emotionally loaded, not sterile.
vocabulary.channeling_vs_creating: Channeling = routing existing demand. Creating = mass education (economically implausible for most).
vocabulary.sentence_chain: Each sentence sells the next. You can say unbelievable things later if copy prepared belief earlier.
